data.title: Cost Discipline And Backlog Wins To Offset DMC Global's Headwinds
data.description:
BOOM Repositions For Long-Cycle Growth

I expect DMC Global (BOOM) to step up on the growth path, despite the 25% decline over the past year. It also underperformed the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) in this period. What ails the recovery of its DynaEnergetics segment is a combination of US land completion activity weakness and typical Q4 energy-market exhaustion. The US onshore drilling and completions activity is softening, led by weaker oil prices and OPEC+ supply push.

Investors may note that the DynaEnergetics segment deals in perforating systems, while NobelClad produces clad metal plates for LNG equipment. A deterioration in the energy environment can affect the BOOM’s DynaEnergetics and NobelClad segments. But we have assured revenue visibility into 2026 from robust backlog growth in Q3. That should allay the fears, at least to a large extent.

I also have concerns over the commercial and high-end residential demand, especially in an uncertain economic environment. Margins, however, should get traction as the management is tightening costs across all segments. Its relative valuation remains below peers, offering upside if EBITDA grows modestly. Backed by higher cash flows, I recommend maintaining the “Hold” call, unchanged from my previous iteration.

End Market Drivers

BOOM’s Arcadia segment results are tied to commercial and high-end residential buildings. Over the past year, the new privately owned housing units declined by 6%. The fall was especially sharp in August 2025. The frac spread count decreased by 14% year-to-date. Demand growth uncertainty and geopolitical concerns have caused market vulnerability in 2025.

Segment Outlook

In the current environment, I see that the DynaEnergetics segment in North America remains under pressure from tariffs and weaker completion activity. It will likely face the usual late-quarter seasonal dip. Arcadia, on the other hand, should hold its own as home building stabilizes. Overall, I think the outlook carries macro risk. But the management is clear that shifting energy markets and tariff swings could push results in either direction. I lean into this dichotomy because I see that US frac spreads have declined by 14% year to date. On the other hand, deepwater and long-cycle, which offer better breakevens and longer reserve lives, present offsetting growth opportunities.

In Q4, BOOM’s management expects sales to land between $140 million and $150 million, or nearly unchanged from Q3. Its adjusted EBITDA can stay within a range of $5 million to $8 million, which would be a 39% fall from Q3. Backlog, however, was strong. It ended the quarter at $57 million, up 53% from Q2, even before adding the follow-on order. NobelClad secured a $20 million order for a petrochemical project in Q3. The order has an option to add another $5 million. This, according to the company, marks one of the most significant awards in several years.

Strategy Updates

BOOM is repositioning Arcadia, DynaEnergetics, and NobelClad through operational improvements, right-sizing, and backlog rebuilding. In Arcadia, it is right-sizing high-end residential offerings. In NobleClad, it looks to maintain operational discipline. So, it can pursue this rather than focusing on production capacity. But its focus goes beyond the income statement. It prioritizes disciplined capital allocation to support margin expansion and cash-flow generation. So, it will tighten its capital structure to improve resilience.

It is paying down debt and shrinking net leverage. The company’s net debt has declined by 47% since the start of the year. It also looks to improve cash generation to fund operations instead of relying on credit. Investors may note that the company’s revolver facility remains undrawn, giving it a cushion during soft quarters in the U.S. onshore.

Near-Term Challenges

It looks like any seasonal slowdown will hit the DynaEnergetics margins because it will stay under pricing pressure, and lower volumes won’t cover fixed manufacturing costs. NobelClad faces the same issue. While the order volume growth has been impressive, it won’t ship until 2026, leaving several quarters of softer absorption.

My Growth Estimates

Over the past 13 quarters, BOOM’s adjusted EBITDA increased by 13% on average. The margin is at a crossroads as opposing forces keep it grounded. While its backlog strength can pull revenues higher, I expect its EBITDA to stay relatively steady due to the challenges I already discussed. I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 6%-8% over the next four quarters.

Segment And Margin Performances In Q3

BOOM’s Q3 earnings report was released on November 4. From Q2 to Q3, NobelClad segment revenues decreased by 21%. I would attribute the fall to softer bookings from earlier quarters and reduced volumes. As volumes shrank, fixed-cost absorption got limited and pulled margins lower. Revenues from the DynaEnergetics segment increased by 3%, while revenues from the Arcadia segment remained unchanged.

If we look closely, Arcadia’s adjusted EBITDA margin improved in Q3. The expansion came from steadier execution and better absorption of fixed costs as stabilization efforts finally yielded results. DynaEnergetics, however, was weighed down by weaker U.S. onshore activity, lower pricing, and tariff-related cost pressure, leading to a lower EBITDA margin in Q3.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

In 9M 2025, BOOM's cash flow from operations, despite lower revenues, increased by 10% compared to a year ago. Free cash flow increased by 18% during this period.

BOOM's net debt was $30 million as of September 30, 2025. Its leverage (debt-to-equity) of 0.22x improved (from 0.28x) at the end of FY2024. The current leverage is also lower than many of its peers.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

BOOM’s forward EV/EBITDA expansion contrasts with its peers (like CLB, HAL, and OIS). That basically means investors are expecting BOOM’s earnings to contract next year, which usually leads to a much lower valuation multiple. Right now, BOOM’s EV/EBITDA is sitting at 3.6x, while its peers average around 8.6x. So, compared to the group, NOA looks to be trading at a reasonable price.

Looking back over the past five years, BOOM’s average multiple was 5.3x. And if it somehow trades at the peer average of 8.6x, we’re talking about a 2.3x upside. As I mentioned earlier, I’m expecting adjusted EBITDA to grow by 4%–6% over the next year. If we plug those numbers into the EV formula and use the current 3.6x multiple, the stock should be trading somewhere between $6.3 and $6.5—which would be around a 14% bump from here.

Analyst Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, one sell-side analyst rated BOOM a "Buy" (including a "Strong Buy"). Two rated it a "Hold," and none rated it a "Sell." The consensus target price is $10.3, which implies an 82% upside at the current price. Given the short-term obstacles to margin growth, I think the sell-side analysts are overestimating the returns from the stock.

What's My Take On BOOM?

Here is what I see in the environment: U.S. onshore remains weak. Offshore and international work continues to build momentum. So, BOOM’s DynaEnergetics segment stays under pressure from tariffs and softer completion activity. The company has activated its strategic activities by reshaping all operating segments through cost resets, tighter capital allocation, and a lighter balance sheet.

By Q3, NobelClad’s significant petrochemical order has added strong visibility. But I do not see the company realizing the benefits until 2026. As a result, its Q4 revenue should be flat. EBITDA, however, can recover partially as the added backlog offers some insulation. I think the seasonal dips and poor absorption remain near-term risks. To its advantage, net debt has dropped sharply this year. And, valuation screens low relative to peers. Considering all the variables, I continue to consider BOOM a “Hold.”

data.title: Enghouse Systems: Intriguing M&A Strategy But Not Enough Yet
data.description:
Enghouse Systems Limited (ENGH:CA) is a software acquirer and integrator company. They focus on vertical markets like communications, transportation, and public safety. Its structure is broadly organized in two segments: 1) the Interactive Management Group (IMG) for contact center, video, and client-engagement tools, and 2) the Asset Management Group for telecom network software, transportation systems, and emergency solutions. Essentially, ENGH purchases profitable companies with recurring income, and then enhances their technology for integrations like adding cloud and AI features. However, for the most part, I don’t think there’s a very compelling opportunity with ENGH at these levels, which is why I deem it a “Hold” for now.

Cloud And AI Rollups

Enghouse Systems Limited is a software company that acquires and develops vertical platforms for communications, transportation, and public-safety solutions. ENGH was founded back in 1984 and is currently headquartered in Markham, Ontario, Canada, with offices in 25 countries. ENGH also grows organically by simply pursuing more software and services clients, while at the same time it seeks opportunistic M&A in complementary software companies to grow repeat revenues.

As a quick overview, ENGH has two business segments. The first one is the Interactive Management Group (IMG), which handles their contact center, video, and marketing solutions. The other segment is Asset Management Group (AMG) for networks, transportation, and public safety.

Moreover, ENGH’s portfolio includes interactive systems that improve customer navigation through automation, visibility in several channels, and analytics. Its solutions cover customer experience (CX), communications, and AI-driven engagement tools, with several categories such as Omni-Channels. This software integrates phone, chat, email, video, and social media in a single interface for agents, with products such as Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS), AI-powered social media management, and secure video.

Another product category within IMG’s solutions is call reception for enterprise-level attendant consoles. This software category includes video interaction, with AI-powered chatbots, and quality management to monitor and analyze agent performance. These solutions provide observations for optimization using AI, and they can offer a bridge to CRM systems. Additionally, IMG provides niche specialty solutions, such as marketing-communication tools, mobile device management, and telecom-expense management.

As for ENGH’s AMG segment, they manage the company’s network portfolio that provides telecom infrastructure and monetization platforms. Its solutions include Business Support Systems (BSS), with Billing-as-a-Service, IoT monetization, and other tools. These services generally help telecoms generate their revenues, which is why I consider ENGH’s role as a third-party B2B service provider of sorts. In addition, AMG offers network infrastructure to manage data and voice traffic, operation support systems (OSS), and platforms for media and TV-as-a-Service.

Growth Through M&A

Interestingly, ENGH’s AMG segment also provides a transportation and public safety portfolio to support operations with mobility, logistics, and emergency systems. Its products cover transit operations and payments for public transportation. On top of that, ENGH covers control systems for first responders, communication tools for operators and dispatchers in logistics, and even fleet management for tracking vehicles and monitoring their performance. This is how ENGH has built, over time, an impressive rollup portfolio of smaller companies. Their latest investor presentation mentioned ENGH typically targets acquisitions in the $5 million to $50 million revenue range.

They also appear to favor acquisitions that are cash flow accretive, with a payback period of 5 to 7 years. But what also stands out is that the AMG portion of their business is quickly becoming their main value driver. You see, ENGH generated CAD $125.6 million in revenues during Q3 2025, which was slightly down 3.8% YoY. However, the AMG segment itself grew 5.6% YoY and contributed CAD $56.0 million in sales. And within AMG, transportation and public safety are increasingly playing a larger role, which management believes is synergistic with their broader SaaS strategy.

Therefore, ENGH’s growth comes from buying existing software companies with proven vertical solutions by targeting recurring revenue, mission-critical applications, and high barriers to entry. And, even though most of ENGH’s products came through M&A, they also have in-house R&D. For example, the company’s engineering teams integrate and develop any acquired software and add features for interoperability or cloud migration. ENGH also does R&D in essential areas like video compression (from Vidyo), network monitoring, and customer service tools leveraging AI.

So, ENGH is not just a holding company that accumulates subsidiaries. They seem to have a more active operational role, mostly focusing on creating synergies throughout their portfolio of companies by sharing all of their technology as appropriate. This know-how facilitates adding value to the solutions provided by the acquired companies. In that sense, in 2025, ENGH acquired three noteworthy software companies. These were Acculab, Margento, and Trafi. Acculab develops telephone and voice-technology solutions, Margento produces mobile payment and smart-ticketing solutions for transport and fintech applications, and Trafi is a Mobility-as-a-Service platform that connects public transport and mobility systems.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ENGH trades at a CAD $1.1 billion market cap (or $786.2 million). Their latest quarterly financials (July 2025) show their balance sheet holds $196.0 million in cash and short-term investments. They don’t have external financial debt, only regular operating liabilities. And their book value reached $435.0 million, indicating a reasonable P/B of 1.8.

Moreover, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on ENGH, the company is projected to reach CAD $513.1 million in revenues by 2026 (or $363.4 million). This would price ENGH at a forward P/S of just 2.1. And I estimate that ENGH generated $19.2 million in quarterly cash flows as of July 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And if we annualize that number, it indicates a yearly cash flow of roughly $76.8 million. In other words, ENGH’s market cap is around 10.2x its annual CF run rate.

That’s why, generally speaking, I think ENGH seems to be about fairly valued at this point. However, I feel I’m not quite sure it fully justifies its risks. For example, I previously mentioned that it’s only its AMG category that’s actually growing. If you look at the IMG segment, this part of their business actually contracted by 10.2% YoY. And, while this is not necessarily a major red flag, it’s still an issue that I think deserves a slightly deeper discount. After all, ENGH’s management frames their strategy as “two-pronged.” But clearly, one of those prongs is declining significantly, which I would say makes it a much riskier investment for long-term investors.

Similarly, the stock itself has been disappointing during 2025, and it's down 24% YTD. And while their dividend yield of 5.8% is attractive on paper, this hasn’t been enough to offset the sell-off in the share price itself. Still, I concede that ENGH has consistently paid a quarterly dividend over the years, which may make it more suitable for income investors. And the quarterly dividend has historically grown over time as well, suggesting management considers this policy a priority.

Besides, as of July 2025, they had 55.1 million shares outstanding, so the current dividend per share of CAD $0.30 represents a cash outflow of roughly CAD $16.5 million per quarter (or $11.7 million). And I think that’s manageable compared to my quarterly cash flow estimate of $19.2 million. Lastly, the stock’s trading volume seems decent with 392.1 thousand shares. At the current CAD $20.00 PPS, it implies a trading liquidity of approximately CAD $7.8 million (or $5.5 million). Hence, despite ENGH being a foreign issuer, slippage costs shouldn’t be a major issue for most retail investors.

Conclusion: Mixed Bag

Overall, I think ENGH has a bit of mixed investment profile. On one hand, I feel their consistent dividends and compelling yield could make it suitable for income investors. And after their recent stock decline, ENGH doesn’t seem wildly overvalued either. However, I also believe ENGH has some serious issues given that one of their strategic prongs (i.e., IMG) is rapidly contracting. Plus, I don’t see a really unique business moat or competitive advantage with ENGH’s underlying prospects. That’s why, on balance, I ultimately lean neutral on the stock at these levels. In my view, ENGH is the type of stock that can make sense at a much deeper discount, but for now, it doesn’t seem like an overly exciting opportunity.

data.title: Rezolve AI PLC: I'd Like To See How The Company Plans To Grow Its ARR To $500m
data.description:
Introduction

Rezolve AI PLC (RZLV) has been doing the rounds recently online, which prompted me to take a look at it in more detail to see what it is all about. The company’s revenue growth has been tremendous and is projected to grow much more in the next year; however, I am a little skeptical of such projections, as they truly seem to be quite unbelievable, so I would like to see more details in the upcoming earnings calls to get a better sense of where the company is heading. Additionally, the company’s share price may again be picked up by a future meme frenzy, which poses extra risks and overvaluation.

However, I do see potential and will be following it along to see how this story plays out.

The Company

Rezolve AI PLC is not to be confused with Resolve AI, which is a completely different company (they should probably do something about it). It is a pure-play AI company that is looking to transform how retail commerce can increase customer engagement through AI-driven search, product discovery, personalized shopping experiences, and checkout solutions. The company aims to help retailers with what they call the best salesperson, their AI, make sales through recommending the best product that the person needs, with much more refined search options and offerings that help retain more customers and keep them coming back.

The company monetizes through a B2B2C approach that targets large e-commerce enterprises, merchants, and payment service providers. The company uses a SaaS business model, charging a monthly fixed fee, with a key operating metric being annual recurring revenue, or ARR for short.

The company’s products include a “Brain Suite” of AI solutions, specifically Brain Commerce, which aims to enhance search accuracy and product discovery, and Brain Checkout, which helps with digital payments and frictionless transactions. These solutions drive increased conversion rates, higher average order values, and customer satisfaction.

The company spiked in popularity recently due to explosive growth in enterprise adoption, having over 100 global clients, like H&M (OTCPK:HNNMY), ASOS Plc (OTCPK:ASOMF), and Urban Outfitters (URBN), as well as notable partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG) that provide cloud infrastructure and co-selling programs.

Briefly on Financials

RZLV recently surprised analysts by beating sales estimates by around $1m, which grew over 425% y/y, coming in at around $6.3m. The company’s Brain Suite products, like Brain Commerce and Brain Checkout, are now live with over 100 enterprise customers around the globe, which helped the company secure over $90m in ARR.

The company is still in its early growth stage, which means it is very unprofitable right now, with G&A expenses ballooning to over $33m, bringing in an operating loss of -$32m for the six months ended June 2025. Additionally, the company’s bottom line suffered even more due to a loss on extinguishment, although these are non-cash charges tied to debt conversions and restructuring. Nevertheless, the company’s GAAP loss came in at around -$0.25. The company’s gross margins for the same period came in at a whopping 95.8% vs. the consensus range of 60%-70%. It seems that analysts don’t overestimate how much the cloud-based SaaS business model requires to run. There’s hardly any cost of sales once the software is delivered to the customer and set up via the cloud. There is very little upkeep and low incremental costs per additional customer. Once everything is set up, the only costs will be compute, storage, and bandwidth, which are not that expensive.

Looking at the company’s financial position, as of the end of June, the company’s cash position stood at around $9.8m, and looking at the company’s debt, most of which is due within a year, it was around $30m. Since the report came out, the company raised $200m in oversubscribed financing, which will easily cover the company’s debt and will help it continue to operate at a loss for a little longer. The company’s cash burn rate is substantial, so future financing arrangements will be required if operations don’t improve dramatically. For the first half of the year, the company’s cash flow was negative $20m, which means that at this rate, there is around $3.3m cash burn per month, giving the company over 5 years of runway. I'm sure that the cash burn rate may continue to increase, so let's call it 2-3 years by being a bit more conservative.

The management emphasized, in the recent conference call, that while reported losses appear high, they are mostly accounting charges and one-offs, so mostly not actual cash charges.

Additionally, with the successful $200m equity financing, the management said that it is confident the company has plenty of "liquidity to support its accelerated scale-up and partnership-driven strategy".

On the same call, the company mentioned a little about its cash burn situation, and just as I said that the cash burn will most likely rise, the management is of the same view, saying, "We anticipate the ongoing cash burn will continue—and potentially rise in the near term—as we invest in team growth, global enterprise expansion, and securing high-value contracts to drive ARR."

Overall, it does seem like quite a risky investment based on the company’s early finances; however, if the company succeeds at attracting more enterprises that are willing to use the company’s proprietary product to enhance customer retention and engagement, these numbers could drastically improve.

Comments on the Outlook

Massive Growth Ahead

A lot of investors are very bullish on the company now that the management said the company will end the year with around $150m in ARR, which is quite an improvement. What is even more impressive is that the management said that the company will achieve around $500m ARR exit rate for 2026. The enterprise partners seem to really like RZLV’s product, and such an increase in ARR in just one year really tells us that the company has been very active in trying to sign up a lot of different enterprises and expand its market reach.

The company is due to report soon; however, there is still no solid date set. I would like to see what kind of numbers the company is going to put up next and whether we will actually see $150m in ARR for this year. If we do, then I would have an easier time believing the company can achieve $500m in ARR for 2026. I am a little bit skeptical right now, but who knows, maybe the company’s product actually helps these enterprises with customer retention and experience that makes them shop more and come back much more often than not.

With such a projected increase in ARR, the company will have to announce quite a few massive partnerships. The company said it anticipates announcing a lot more new partnerships and collaborations globally. With the collaborations with Google and Microsoft under its belt and the recent $10m contract with Mexico's Liverpool department store chain, the company is paving the way to move across the globe, and I expect the company not to lose momentum.

The company, with such a portfolio of companies behind it, has a decent leg to stand on, which demonstrates that there is potential, which is valuable for these department store chains and the like. I wouldn't be surprised if the company announces some massive partnerships with some huge retail store chains in the future in the US, Mexico, and Europe, since they already have a foothold in these regions. The execution of it will be vital for the company to sustain its operations.

One of the company’s product features is its Agentic AI, which is becoming quite a big buzz around the world of technology. I am currently using an agentic AI for some app building, and I find the overall experience decent, although maybe slightly expensive, especially since, after using it for many months now, I find it to be not as good as I hoped. Oftentimes, the AI agent would get stuck in a sort of a loop and not provide me with any reasonable solution, which in turn cost me money. If the chat window has been open for too long, it could start misbehaving and offer solutions out of thin air with a back of how they got there.

Rezolve’s brainpowa AI engine is claimed to achieve near 0% hallucination, and I, for one, find this to be questionable. This is a common challenge in general for LLMs like ChatGPT and Gemini. How is the company managing such a feat? Well, from what I gathered, the engine is trained on specific eCommerce data, with domain-specific data focused strictly on retail product information and transactional context. They continue to fine-tune and validate against real-world retail scenarios to correct any inaccuracies, as well as they incorporate built-in verification layers that cross-check AI responses with the verified product databases. Since it is being trained on a lot more specific data, I can see the hallucination rates being this low. It has been working out so far, and it would be interesting to see what happens when the company enters into a partnership with a much bigger retailer.

In terms of the company’s ability to grow, seeing such healthy, near 100% gross margins, I can see there is a lot of potential to scale the business going forward. These types of margins will allow the company to become profitable once the aggressive growth phase subsides or once the company reaches a lot more in ARR. If the company reaches $500m in ARR by the end of 2026, would this be enough to finally turn a profit? At least on non-GAAP terms? It’s hard to tell given the massive growth in operating expenses right now, but if ARR continues to grow like this, there is a huge possibility the company will turn positive on paper.

What Worries Me Right Now

The biggest risks that I can see right now are some operational and some social.

Operationally speaking, since the company is being very aggressive in its growth right now, expenses are piling up, the company is deeper in the red than before, and the need to continue to finance this growth massively dilutes current shareholders. If we look at the most recent report, we can see that the company’s share count went from around 151m in June ’24 to 233m in June ’25. This is before the company secured an additional $200m in oversubscribed financing, which means there are going to be a lot more shares on the following report. Looking at the 6-K form for the offering, the company is issuing 37m shares at $5.40 per share. It is a massive dilution, but I think it is necessary, especially in such early stages. The company’s share price appreciation could easily outweigh the share dilution, but if that doesn’t materialize, then it is a real risk.

Potential Meme Stock

I’ve been lurking around Reddit’s (RDDT) many subreddits regarding finance and stocks and the like, and there have been quite a few times that I have seen Rezolve’s name popping up. I believe just yesterday I saw a person going all in on RZLV with little to no DD, other than the whole “I like the stock” shtick. These types of posts are common and, I must say, are quite entertaining, but they are not good for a company that is not trying to look like a meme and is trying to grow as a real business. The recent Beyond Meat (BYND) drama spawned many subreddits and cult-like behavior, which grew old real fast, and yet there are still rumblings of diamond hands for BYND stock. Rezolve could see yet another spike in its share price, as it did back in late August to mid-September. Since then, the company’s share price has plummeted, which will most likely lead to another meme frenzy in the near future, especially when the company is going to report its numbers.

Investor Takeaway

In my view, there is something here, if all the projected numbers are to be believed. The company is experiencing fantastic growth, but that isn’t coming without risks. If the company cannot convert more enterprises into using the company’s products while its expenses continue to balloon, further share price dilution to finance the company’s operations is inevitable.

I am going to hold off on recommending the company to buy at this stage, mainly because the claims of such ARR are a little hard to believe, and I would like to see this story come closer to the truth. The upcoming quarter could give us a better glimpse into the company’s future outlook regarding new partnerships that are going to fuel such growth in ARR.

I would like to see this company forgotten by the many subreddits so that the company’s share would better align with its operations.

Additionally, I already own a company in my portfolio that is rather risky and may be seen as even more risky than RZLV since it is still a pre-revenue company, so I am not looking to add another high-risk play for now but will be following closely to see how this story develops.
